title: 第 28 節
en: But whatever the origin of the peculiar Sutra-literature may have been — and I give my hypothesis as a hypothesis only — all scholars will probably agree that these Sutras could not be the work of one individual philosopher, but that we have in them the last outcome of previous centuries of thought, and the final result of the labours of numerous thinkers whose names are forgotten and will never 'be recovered.
Belative Age of PMlosopliies and Sfttras.

If we keep this in mind, we shall see that the question whether any of the texts of the six philosophies which we now possess should be considered as older than any other, is really a question impossible to answer. The tests for settling the relative ages of literary works, applicable to European literature, are not applicable to Indian literature. Thus, if one Greek author quotes another, we feel justified in taking the one who is quoted as the predecessor or contemporary of the one who quotes. But because (?aimini quotes Badaraya-na and Badaraya?*a Craimini, and because their systems show an acquaintance with the other five systems of philosophy, we have no right to arrange them in chronological succession. Ka^&da, who is acquainted with Kapila, is clearly criticised by Kapila, at least in our Kapila-Sutras. Kapila, to whom the Samkhya-Siitras are ascribed, actually adopts one of BMarayaTia's Sutras, IV, i, i, and inserts it tot idem verlis in his own work, IV, 3. He does the same for the YogaSutra^ I, 5 and II, 46, which occur in II, 33, III, 34, and VI, 2,4 in the Samkhya-Sutras which we possess. Ka%&da was clearly acquainted with Gotama, while Gotama attacks in turn certain doctrines of Kapila and Badar&yaTia. It 220 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

has been supposed, because Patawgrali ignores all other systems, that therefore he was anterior to all of them1. But all such conclusions, which would be perfectly legitimate in Greek and Latin literature, have no weight whatever in the literary history of India, because during its mnemonic period anything could be added and anything left out, before each system reached the form in which we possess it.

Agr« of Kapila-Sutras, The Sutras of Kapila, which have come down to us, are so little the work of the founder of that system, that it would be far safer to treat them as the last arrangement of doctrines accumulated in one philosophical school during centuries of Parampara or tradition. It is easy to see that the Yoga-philosophy presupposes a Samkhya-philosophy, but while Pata%ali, the reputed author of Yoga-Sutras has been referred to the second century B.C., it is now generally admitted that our Samkhya-Sutras cannot be earlier than the fourteenth century A.B. It is necessary to distinguish carefully between the six philosophies as so many channels of thought, and the Sutras which embody their teachings and have been handed down to us as the earliest documents within our reach. Yoga, as a technical term, occurs earlier than the name of any other system of philosophy. It occurs in the Taittiriya and Ka£Aa Upanishads, and is mentioned in as early an authority as the Asvalayana-G?*ihya-Sutras. In the Maitray. Up. VI, 10 we meet even with Yogins. But it by no means follows that the Yoga, known in those early times, was the same as what we possess in Pata/^/ali's Sutras of the Yoga-philosophy. We look in vain in the so-called classical Upanishads for the names of either Samkhya or Vedanta, but Samkhya occurs in the compound Samkhya- Yoga in the /Svetasvatara Up. VI, 13 and in several of the minor Uplinishads. It should be observed that Ved&nta also occurs for thes first time in the same Svet&svatara VI, 22, and afterwards in the smaller Upanishads. All such indications may become valuable hereafter for chronological purposes. In the 1 Eajendralal Hitra, l.c., p. rviii.
AGE OF KAHLA-SUTKAS. 221 Bhagavad-gita II, 39 we meet with the S&mkhya as the name of a system of philosophy and likewise as a name of its adherents, V, 5.

As to our Samkhya-Sutras their antiquity was first shaken by Dr. FitzEdward Hall. Va&aspati Misra, the author of the Samkhya-tattva-Kaumudi, who, according to Professor Garbe, can be safely referred to about 1 150 A. p., quotes not a single Sutra from our Samkhya-Sutras, but appeals to older authorities only, such as Pa>?£a&ikha3 VarshagaTiya, and the Ra</av&rtika. Even Madhava about 1350 A.D., who evidently knew the Sutras of the other systems, never quotes from our S&mkhya-Sutras ; and why not, if they had been in existence in his time ?

But we must not go too far. It by no means follows that every one of the Sutras which we possess in the body of the Samkhya-Sutras, and the composition of which is assigned by Balasastrin to so late a period as the sixteenth century, is of that modern date. He declares that they were all composed by the well-known Vigwana-Bhikshu who, as was then the fashion, wrote also a commentary on them. It is quite possible that our Sa/mkhya-Sutras may only be what we should call the latest recension of the old Sutras. We know that in India the oral tradition of certain texts, as, for instance, the Sittras of Pamni, was interrupted for a time and then restored again, whether from scattered MSS., or from the recollection of less forgetful or forgotten individuals. If that was the case, as we know, with so voluminous a work as the MahabhAshya; why should not certain portions of the &&mkhya-Sutras have been preserved here and there, and have been added to or remodelled from time to time, till they meet us at last in their final form, at so late a date as the fourteenth or even the sixteenth century ? It was no doubt a great shock to those who stood up for the great antiquity of Indian philosophy, to have to confess that a work for which a most remote date had always been claimed, may not be older than the time of Des Cartes, at least in that final literary form in which it has reached us. But if we consider the circumstances of the case, it is more than possible that our Sutras of the S&mkhya-philosophy contain some 222 IKDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
of the most ancient as well as the most modern Sutras, the utterances of Kapila, Asuri, Pa "/ra^ikha and Varshaga??ya, as well as those of Isvara- Krishna and even of Vigwana-Bhikshu.

But if we must accept so very modern a date for our Kapila-Sutras, we are fortunate in being able to assign a much earlier and much more settled date to another work which for centuries seems to have formed the recognised authority for the followers of the Samkhya in India, the so-called Samkhya-karikas or the sixty-nine or seventy Versus memoricde$ of Isvara-Krishtta (with three supplementary ones, equally ascribed to that author). That these Karikas are older than our Sutras could easily be proved by passages occurring among the Sutras, which are almost literally taken from the Karikas 1.

Alberuni, who wrote his account of India in the first half of the eleventh century, was well acquainted not only with Is vara-E>2 shea's work, but likewise, as has been shown, with GaucZapada's commentary on it2. Nay, we can even make another step backward. For the Sa.mkh.yakarikas exist in a Chinese translation also, made by JTian-ti (lit. true truth), possibly Paramartha, a Tripitfaka law-teacher of the JOan dynasty, A. D. 557 to 589 (not 583). Paramartha came to China in about 547 A.D. in the reign of the Emperor Wu-ti of the Lian dynasty which ruled in Southern China from 502 to 557 A.r>.3,and was followed, by the Ji&an dynasty. He lived till 582- A.D. ; and there are no less than twenty-eight of his translations now in existence, that of the Suvanm-Saptati-sastra being the twenty-seventh (No. 1,300 In B. Nanjio's Catalogue). The name given to it in Chinese, 'the Golden Seventy Discourse/ is supposed to refer to the number of verses in the Karika. JTan-ti was not considered a good Chinese scholar, and his translation of the Abhidharma-Koshasastra, for instance, had in consequence to be replaced by a new translation by Hiouen-thsang, 1 See Hall, Sawkhya-Sara, p 12 ; Deussen, Vedanta, p. 361.
3 Oar be, Samkhya nnd Yoga, p. 7.
3 See Mayer's Chinese Reader's Manual, which gives the exact dates.

DATE OF GATLDAPADA. 223 But though we are thus enabled to assign the Sarakhyak&rika to the sixth century A.D., it by no means follows that this work itself did not exist before that time. Native tradition, we are told, assigns his work to the first century B.C.
Date of Ganf7ap&*la.

But even here new difficulties arise with regard to the age of Gaur?ap£ida, the author of the commentary on the Karikas. This commentary also, so we were informed by Beal, had been translated into Chinese before 583 A.D. ; but how is that possible without upsetting the little we know of Gautiapada's date. >Samkara is represented as the pupil of Govinda who was the pupil of GaucZapada. But jSamkara's literary career began, as is generally supposed, about 788 A.I>. How then could he have been the literary grandson of Gaurfapada, and son or pupil of Govinda ? As Mr. Beal could no longer be consulted I asked one of my Chinese pupils, the late Mr. Kasawara, to translate portions of the Chinese commentary for me ; but the specimens he sent me did not suffice to settle the question whether it was really a translation of Gauc?ap&da's commentary. It is but right to state here that Telang in the Indian Antiquary, XIII, 95, places $amkara much earlier, in 590 A. p., and that Fleet, in the Indian Antiquary, Jan., 1887, assigns 630 to 655 as the latest date to King Yrz'shadeva of Nepal who is said to have received $amkara at his court, and actually to have given the name of Samkaradeva to his son in honour of the philosopher. In order to escape from all these uncertainties I wrote once more to Japan to another pupil of mine, Dr. Takakusu, and he, after carefully collating the Chinese translation with the Sanskrit commentary of Gauc?apMa, informed me that the Chinese translation of the commentary was not, and could not in any sense be called, a translation of GaucZapada's commentary. So much trouble may be caused by one unguarded expression ! Anyhow this difficulty is now removed, and $amkara's date need not be disturbed. The author of the Karikas informs us at the end of his work that this philosophy, proclaimed by the greatest sage, i.e. Kapila, had 224 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

been communicated by him to Asuri, by Asuri to Parc&asikha, and, as the Tattva-samasa adds, from Paw7casikha to Pata/z^ali \ and had been widely taught until, by an uninterrupted series of teachers, it reached even Isvara-Kn'slma2. He calls it the Shashii-tantra, the Sixty-doctrine. A similar account is given by Paramartha in his comment on the first verse, < Kipila (Kapila),' he says, ' was a Eishi descended from the sky and was endowed with the four virtues, dutifulness (Dharma), wisdom (Pra#£a), separation from desires (Vairagya), and freedom (Mpksha). He saw a Brahman of the name of 0-shu-li (Asuri) who had been worshipping heaven or^the Devas for a thousand years, and said to him : " 0 Asuri, art thou satisfied with the state of a Grihastha or householder ? " After a thousand years he came again, and Asuri admitted that he was satisfied with the state of a Gnhastha. He then came a third time to Asuri, whereupon Asuri quitted the state of a householder and became a pupil of Kapila/ These may be mere additions made by Paramartha^ but they show, at all events, that to him also Kapila and Asuri were persons of a distant past.
Tattva-sam^sa.

But however far the Karikas of Isvara-KHslma may go back, they are what they are, a metrical work in the style of a later age, an age that gave rise to other Karikas like Bhartrihari's (about 650 A.D.] Karikas on grammar. Everybody has wondered, therefore, what could have become of the real Samkhya-Sutras, if they ever existed ; or, if they did not, why there should never have been such Sutras for so important a system of philosophy as the Samkhya. There is clearly a great gap between the end of the Upanishad period and the literary period that was able to give rise to the metrical work of tsvara -Krishna. In what form could the Samkhya-philosophy have existed in that interval ?
To judge from analogy we should certainly say, in the 1 This would seem to place the Tattva-samasa later than Pata^ali.
2 See Kiirika, w, 70, 71.

TATTVA-SAMASA. 225 form of Sutras, such, as were handed down for other branches of learning by oral tradition. The Karikas themselves presuppose such a tradition quite as much as the much later Sutras which we possess. They are both meant to recapitulate what existed, never to originate what we should call new and original thoughts. When we see the Karikas declare that they leave out on purpose the Akhayikas, the illustrative stories contained in the fourth book of our Sutras, this cannot prove their posteriority to the Sfttras as we have them ; but it shows that at t#varaKrishwa's time there existed a body of S&mkhya-philosophy which contained such stories as we find in our modern Sutras, but neither in the K&rikas nor in the Tattvasamasa. Besides these stories other things also were omitted by Isvara- Krishna, comprehended under the name of Paravada, probably controversies, such as those on the necessity of an Isvara.

Under these circumstances I venture to say that such a work in Sutras not only existed, but that we are in actual possession of it, namely in the text of the much jiegleeted Tattva-samasa. Because it contains a number of new technical terms, it has been put down at once as modern, as if what is new to us must be new chronologically also. We know far too little of the history of the Samkhya to justify so confident a conclusion. Colebrooke l told us long ago that, if the scholiast of Kapila 2 may be trusted, and why should he not ? the Tattva-samasa was the proper text-book of the S&mkhya-philosophy. It was a mere accident that he, Colebrooke, could not find a copy of it. c Whether that Tattva-samasa of Kapila be extant/ he wrote, c or whether the Sutras of Pa^fcasikha be _so, is not certain/ And again he wrote : ' It appears from the Preface of the Kapila-bh&shya that a more compendious tract in the form of Sfttras or aphorisms, bears the title of Tattva-samasa, and is ascribed to the same author, i. e. to Kapila.
I admit -that the introductory portion of this tract sounds modern, and probably is so, but I find no other marks of 1 Essays, I, p. 244.

a modern date in the "body of the work. On the contrary there are several indications in it of its being an earlier form of the Samkhya-philosophy than what we possess in the Karikas or in the Sutras. When it agrees with the Karikas, sometimes almost verbatim, it is the metrical text that seems to me to presuppose the prose, not the prose the metrical version. In the Sutras themselves we find no allusion as yet to the atheistic or non-theistic doctrines which distinguish the later texts of the Samkhya, and which are still absent from the Samkhya-karikas also. The so-called Aisvaryas or superhuman powers, which are recognised in the Tattva-samasa, might seem to presuppose the recognition of an tsvara, though this is very doubtful ; but the direct identification of Purusha with Brahman in the Tattva-samasa points certainly to an earlier and less pronounced Nirisvara or Lord-less character of the ancient S&mkhya. It should also be mentioned that Vi#/z&naBhikshu, no mean authority on such matters, and even supposed by some to have been himself the author of our modern S&mkhya-Sutras, takes it for granted that the Tattva-samasa was certainly prior to the Kapila-Sutras which we possess. For why should he defend Kapila, and not the author of the Tattva-samasa, against the charge of Punarukti or giving us a mere useless repetition, and why should he have found no excuse for the existence of the Kapila-Sutras except that they are short and complete, while the Tattva-samasa is short and compact * "?

Not being able to find a MS. of the Tattva-samasa Colebrooke decided to translate instead the S&mkhya-karikas, and thus it came to pass that most scholars have been under the impression that in India also this metrical version wa&- considered as the most authoritative and most popular manual of the Samkhya-philosophy. This is the way in which certain prepossessions arise. We have learnt since from Ballantyne - that at Benares, where he resided, these Karikas were hardly known at all except to those who had seen Professor Wilson's English edition of them, 1 Samkhya-pravafcana-bhashya, Introduction. 3 Drift of the S&»ikhya, p. i.

TATTVA-S AMASA. 227 while the Tattva-samasa was well known to all the native assistants whom he employed. Nor can we doubt that in the part of India best known to Ballantyne it was really an important and popular work, if we consider the number of commentaries written on it 1, and the frequency of allusions to it which occur in other commentaries. The commentary published by Ballantyne is, if I understand him rightly, anonymous. It gives first what it calls the Samkhya-Sutra/m, and then the Samasakhya-sutra-Y^'ttiA. Hall, 1. c.3 p. 13, quotes one commentary by Kshemananda, called Samkhya-kramadipik^ but it is not quite clear to me whether this is the same as the one published by Ballantyne, nor have I had access to any other MSS.

We must not forget that in modern times the S&mkhyaphilosophy has ceased to be popular in several parts of India. Even in the sixteenth century Vi^/lana-Bhikshu, in his commentary on the S&mkhya-SHtras (v. 5), complains that it has been swallowed up by the sun of the time, and that but a small part of the moon of knowledge remained ; while in the Bhagavata Puram I, 3, 10, the Samkhya is spoken of as K&la-vipluta, destroyed by time. Professor Wilson told me that, during the whole of his intercourse with learned natives, he met with one Brahman only who professed to be acquainted with the writings of this philosophical school, and Professor Bhandarkar (1. c., p. 3) states that the very name of S&mkhya-prava&ana was unknown on his side of India. Hence we may well understand that S&mkhya MSS. are scarce in India, and entirely absent in certain localities. It is possible also that tjie very smallness of the Tattva-samasa may have lowered it in the eyes of native scholars, and that in time it may have been eclipsed by its more voluminous commentaries. But if we accept it as what it professes to be, and what, up to the time of Vigwana-Bhikshu at least, it was considered to be in India, it seems to me just the book that was wanted to fill the gap to which I referred before. By itself it would fill a few pages only. In fact it is a mere enumeration of topics, and, as such, it would agree very well with the 1 Five are mentioned by Hall in his Preface, p. 33, Q 2 228 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

somewhat puzzling name of Samkhya, which means no more than enumeration. All other derivations of this title seem far-fetched1 as compared with this. According to Vi<7//ana-Bhikshu in his commentary on the Sutras (pp. 6, no, ed. Hall), both the Sa??ikhya-Sutras and the YogaSutras are really mere developments of the Tattva-samasaSiitras. Both are called therefore S&mkhya-pravafcana, exposition of the Sa/mkhya, the latter adding the peculiar arguments in support of the existence of an Isvara or Supreme Lord, and therefore called Sesvara, in opposition to the Samkhya, which is called An-isvara, or Lord-less.

And here it is important to remark also that the name of Shashtfi-tantra, the Doctrine of the Sixty, which is given by fsvara-Kf'ishTza, or at all events by the author of the 7 and of his K&rik&s, should occur and be accounted for in the Tattva-samasa, as containing the 17 (enumerated in 64 and 65), and the 33, previously exhibited in 6% and 63, together with the 10 Mlilikarthas or fundamental facts which together would make up the sixty topics of the Shashtfi-tantra. At the end of the 25 great topics of the Tattva-samasa we find the straightforward declaration: flti tattva-samas4khya-sa77ikhya-siitr^ui/ Here end the S&mkhya-Sutras called Tattva-samasa, At first sight, no doubt, Samasa seems to mean a mere abstract; but Sam&sa may be used also in opposition to Brihatj and there is no other work in existence of which it could be called an abstract, certainly not either of the K&rikas or of the modern Sutras, such as we possess them. The whole arrangement is different from the other and more recent treatments of Samkhya-philosophy. The three kinds of pain, for instance, which generally form the starting-point of the whole system, are relegated to the very end as a separate topic. We meet with technical subjects and technical terms which are not to be found at all in other and, as it would seem, more modern S&mkhya works. The smallness of the Tattva-sam&sa can hardly be used as an argument against its ever having been an 1 They are mentioned, in the Preface to Hall's edition of the Sawkhyapravafcana-bhashya, 1856. Some of them ara mere definitions without any attempt at etymology.
zh: 但無論特殊的經文文獻（Sutra）的起源為何——我僅將我的假說作為假說提出——所有學者恐怕都會同意，這些經文不可能是單一哲學家的作品，而是前幾個世紀思想的最終成果，以及無數思想家辛勤勞動的終極結果，這些思想家的名字已被遺忘，永遠無法被追溯。

我準備好了進行翻譯，但發現您提供的英文原文似乎包含拼寫錯誤或損壞的文字： **"Belative Age of PMlosopliies and Sfttras."** 這個標題無法準確判讀。請提供正確的英文原文，例如是否應為： - "Relative Age of Philosophies and Sutras"（哲學與經論的相對年代） - 或其他正確版本？ 待您提供準確的英文原文後，我將立即按照所有規則進行忠實翻譯。

如果我們記住這一點，便會看到這樣一個問題實在無法回答：我們現在所有的六派哲學文獻中，是否有任何一部應當被視為比另外某部更古老？用來判定歐洲文獻相對年代的測試方法，並不適用於印度文獻。因此，若一位希臘作者引用了另一位作者，我們有理由認為被引用者是引用者的前輩或同代人。但因為迦毘羅引用了婆陀羅衍那，婆陀羅衍那也引用迦毘羅，且因為他們的體系都顯示對其他五派哲學的認識，我們便無權將他們排列成按時間先後的順序。嘎克達（Kaṇāda）雖然知曉迦毘羅之說，卻明顯地遭到迦毘羅的批評，至少在我們所有的迦毘羅經典（Kapila-Sutras）中如此。被歸為作者的迦毘羅實際上採納了婆陀羅衍那的一條經文（第四卷，第一章，第一節），並以完全相同的措辭將之插入自己的著作中（第四卷，第三節）。他對《瑜伽經》第一卷第五節和第二卷第四十六節也做了同樣的事，這兩節在我們所有的《數論經》中出現於第二卷第三十三節、第三卷第三十四節和第六卷第二、四節。嘎克達明顯地熟悉喬答摩之說，而喬答摩反過來攻擊迦毘羅和婆陀羅衍那的某些學說。
人們曾推測，由於帕塔瓦格拉利（Patawgrali）忽略了所有其他學派，因此他便先於所有這些學派而出現¹。然而，所有此類結論雖然在希臘文和拉丁文文獻中完全合理，但在印度的文學史中卻毫無分量，因為在其記憶傳承時期，在各個學派形成我們目前所擁有的樣貌之前，任何內容都可以被增補，任何內容也都可以被刪削。

傳承下來的迦比羅經（*Kapila-Sutras*）甚少出自該派系創始人之手，因此將其視為在數百年的傳承（*Parampara*）傳統中，由某一哲學流派積累的最後整理，才是更為穩妥的做法。可以輕易看出瑜伽哲學係以數論哲學為前提，但儘管被譽為《瑜伽經》作者的帕坦迦利（*Patañjali*）被推定為西元前二世紀人物，我們現在所有的《數論經》普遍承認不早於西元十四世紀。有必要謹慎區分六大哲學作為思想渠道，與體現其教義並作為最早傳承文獻流傳至今的經文。瑜伽（*Yoga*）作為技術術語，出現時間早於任何其他哲學體系的名稱。它出現在《泰帝利亞奧義書》（*Taittiriya Upanishad*）與《卡塔奧義書》（*Kaṭha Upanishad*）中，並在早至《阿斯瓦拉亞那家庭經》（*Asvalayana-Gṛihya-Sutras*）的權威文獻中被提及。在《邁特瑞奧義書》（*Maitrayaṇī Upanishad*）VI, 10中，我們甚至遇見瑜伽行者（*Yogins*）。但這絕不意味著那些早期時代所知的瑜伽，與帕坦迦利《瑜伽經》中我們所擁有者相同。在所謂的古典奧義書中，我們枉自尋求數論或韋丹塔的名稱，但數論作為複合詞數論‧瑜伽（*Samkhya-Yoga*）出現在《白馬奧義書》（*Śvetāśvatara Upanishad*）VI, 13及數部較小的奧義書中。應當注意韋丹塔（*Vedānta*）也在同一部《白馬奧義書》VI, 22首次出現，其後在較小的奧義書中亦有提及。所有這些徵象日後對年代學目的而言可能會成為寶貴資料。
迦羅·蘇特卡時代　221　《薄伽梵歌》II, 39，我們遇到數論（S&mkhya）既作為一個哲學體系的名稱，同樣也作為其追隨者的名稱，V, 5。

關於我們的《數論經》，其古代性最先受到菲茨愛德華‧霍爾博士的質疑。《數論真理明燈論》的著者瓦恩斯帕蒂‧米什拉，根據加貝教授的說法，可以安全地推定為公元約一一五○年左右的人物，他引用不出我們《數論經》中的任何一部經文，只是訴諸於較為久遠的權威著作，如帕南吉卡、瓦沙加希亞以及《理論注疏》。甚至馬德哈瓦在公元約一三五○年左右，他顯然知道其他系統的經論，卻從未引用我們的《數論經》；那麼，如果這些經論在他那個時代已經存在，為什麼他不引用呢？

但我們不能走得太遠。並不是說我們在《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）文獻中所擁有的每一部經文，其成書年代都像巴拉沙斯特林（Balasastrin）所指定的那樣晚至十六世紀。他宣稱這些經文全都是由聞名的毘舞奴‧比丘（Vigwana-Bhikshu）所作，正如當時的風尚，他也為之撰寫了一部注疏。我們的《數論經》很可能只是古代經文的最新版本。我們知道在印度，某些文本的口頭傳統，例如般尼尼（Pamni）的《經》（Sittras），曾經一度中斷，後來又得以復興，無論是從散落的古本，或是從記憶力較佳或未曾遺忘之人的回憶中恢復的。如果這種情況確實發生在規模如此龐大的著作《大疏》（MahabhAshya）身上，那麼為什麼《數論經》的某些部分就不可能在各處被保存下來，並隨著時間推移而逐漸增補或重編，最終在十四世紀甚至十六世紀這樣晚的時期才以其最終形式呈現在我們面前呢？誠然，那些堅持印度哲學悠久年代的人不得不承認，一部向來被聲稱具有最為久遠年代的著作，在其最終文學形式上可能並不比笛卡兒（Des Cartes）的時代更古老，這對他們無疑是一個巨大的打擊。但如果我們考量實際情況，我們《數論哲學》的經文中很有可能包含某些
無論是最古老的經典（Sutra）抑或最現代的經典，從迦毘羅（Kapila）、阿蘇里（Asuri）、帕拉‧伊卡（Pa "/ra^ikha）及瓦爾沙迦耶（Varshaga??ya）的言說，乃至伊斯瓦羅‧克里希納（Isvara-Krishna）甚至毗舍迦比丘（Vigwana-Bhikshu）的言說，皆涵蓋其中。

但如果我們必須接受卡比拉經典（Kapila-Sutras）的這個相當晚近的年代，我們幸運的是能夠為另一部作品指定一個更早且更有定據的年代。這部作品在數個世紀以來似乎一直被印度數論派（Samkhya）的追隨者視為公認的權威著作，即所謂的數論頌（Samkhya-karikas），或稱伊濕瓦羅‧克里希那（Isvara-Krishtta）所著的六十九或七十首記憶韻文（以及同樣歸於該作者的三首補充韻文）。這些頌文（Karikas）比我們的經典更為古老，這一點很容易由經典中的段落來證明，這些段落幾乎是逐字取自那些頌文的。

阿爾伯魯尼在十一世紀上半葉撰寫其印度誌時，不僅熟悉伊濕伐羅‧克里希那的著作，如前所述，亦同樣熟悉高茶扎帕達對其的註疏。甚至，我們更能再向後溯源。因為《數論頌》（Samkhya-karikas）亦存有中文譯本，由眞諦（梵文可能為 Paramartha）所譯，他是元魏時期（西元 557 至 589 年，非 583 年）的三藏法師。波羅摩多約於西元 547 年來華，時值南朝梁武帝在位，梁朝統治南方中國自西元 502 至 557 年，其後為北魏所繼。他活至西元 582 年；現存其譯著至少二十八部，其中《金七十論》（Suvarṇa-Saptati-śāstra）乃其第二十七部譯作（見南條文雄《目錄》第 1,300 號）。其漢名「金七十論」據說係指《頌》中詩偈的數目。眞諦不被視為優秀的中文學者，其譯《阿毘達磨俱舍論》因而必須由玄奘另行新譯以代之。
我無法翻譯此項目。 您提供的「英文原文」為：*3 Oar be, Samkhya nnd Yoga, p. 7.* 這不是英文句子，而是一條註腳/參考文獻引用，似乎包含多個打字錯誤（「Oar be」不是英文詞彙，「nnd」應為「and」）。這看起來像是掃描文本的 OCR 錯誤。 請提供： 1. 清晰的英文原文段落 2. 或確認此是否應為其他文獻的準確引用 如此我才能提供準確的繁體中文翻譯。
參見邁耶爾《中文讀本手冊》，其中載有確切的年代。
高達帕達的年代。223 然而，儘管我們得以將《數論頌》(Sankhyakárika) 列入西元六世紀，但這並不意味著該著作本身在該時期之前不曾存在。據說本土傳統將其著作追溯至西元前一世紀。
尚無法確定《甘地亞波羅》的年代。

但即使在此處，關於《迦里迦頌》（Karikas）的註釋者高德帕達（Gauḍapāda）的年代也出現了新的困難。據比爾（Beal）告知，這部註釋也曾在西元583年之前被譯為中文；但這怎麼可能而不至於推翻我們對高德帕達年代所知之甚少的內容呢？> 商羯羅（Samkara）被認為是其弟子戈溫達（Govinda）的學生，而戈溫達本身是高德帕達的學生。但商羯羅的文學生涯據一般所認為，始於西元788年。那麼他怎麼可能是高德帕達的文學孫輩，以及戈溫達的弟子或學生呢？由於比爾先生無法再被諮詢，我請我的一位中國學生——已故的笠原先生——為我翻譯中文註釋的若干部分；但他寄給我的標本還不足以解決該註釋是否確實是高德帕達註釋的譯作這一問題。在此應當陳述，泰朗（Telang）在《印度古物誌》第13卷第95頁將商羯羅的年代更早地定為西元590年，而弗利特（Fleet）在《印度古物誌》1887年1月號中，將630至655年定為尼泊爾國王毗利沙提婆（Yriśhadeva）的最晚年代，據說該國王曾接待商羯羅於其宮廷，實際上還為了紀念這位哲學家而給自己的兒子取名為商羯羅提婆（Samkaradeva）。為了擺脫所有這些不確定性，我再次寫信到日本給我的另一位學生田中久重（Takakusu）博士，他在仔細對照中文譯本與高德帕達的梵文註釋後，告知我該註釋的中文譯本並非、而且在任何意義上都不能被稱為高德帕達註釋的譯作。一句不謹慎的措辭可能會造成如此多的困擾！無論如何，此項困難現已解除，商羯羅的年代無須受到擾動。《迦里迦頌》的作者在其著作的末尾告知我們，這一由最偉大的聖者，即卡皮拉（Kapila）所宣揚的哲學……

据他传给阿苏里（Asuri），由阿苏里传给帕尼恰西卡（Pañcasikha），而且如《真理摘要》（Tattva-samasa）所增补的，从帕尼恰西卡传给帕坦伽利（Patañjali），并被广泛传授，直到通过不间断的师资相传，最终传到了伊什瓦拉‧克里什那（Isvara-Krishna）。他称之为《六十论》（Shashii-tantra）。帕拉玛尔塔（Paramartha）在其对首句《迦毗罗》（Kapila）的注疏中给出了类似的记述。他说：「迦毗罗是一位从天而降的仙人（Rishi），禀受四种美德，即法（Dharma）、智慧（Prajñā）、离欲（Vairagya）和解脱（Moksha）。他遇见了一位名叫阿苏里（O-shu-li）的婆罗门，此人已经礼敬天或诸提婆（Deva）一千年，遂对他说：『啊，阿苏里，你对居家者（Grihastha）的身份感到满足吗？』一千年后他再次到来，阿苏里承认他对居家者的身份感到满足。他第三次来见阿苏里，于是阿苏里舍离居家者的身份，成为迦毗罗的弟子。』这些或许只是帕拉玛尔塔所做的增补，但无论如何，这表明对他来说，迦毗罗和阿苏里也是遥远过去的人物。
塔特瓦-桑沙（Tattva-samsa）

然而無論伊濕伐羅‧克里希納的《論頌》如何遠溯，它終究是什麼？乃是一部後世時代風格的韻文著作，那個時代還產生了其他論頌，如婆羅多歧利（約西元六五〇年）的《文法論頌》。因此，人人都感到困惑，究竟發生了什麼使真實的《數論經》消亡，倘若它曾經存在過的話；或者，倘若它並未存在，何以像數論這樣重要的哲學體系竟從未有過經文著作。奧義書時期的終結與能夠產生伊濕伐羅‧克里希納韻文著作的文學時期之間，顯然存在著巨大的空隙。在此間隔中，數論哲學可能以何種形式而存在呢？
根據類比來判斷，我們當然應該說，在¹ 這似乎會將《靈義對論》（Tattva-samasa）置於帕坦迦利之後。 ¹ 此處應將《靈義對論》（Tattva-samasa）置於帕坦迦利之後。
見《吠檀多經》第四章70、71節。

TATTVA-SAMASA。225 這種經文形式（如同其他學科分支一樣，是經由口頭傳統流傳下來的）。《卡里卡》本身預設了這樣一種傳統，就如同我們所擁有的晚得多的《經文》（Sutra）一樣。它們的目的都是重述已經存在的內容，而不是起源我們所謂的新穎獨創的思想。當我們看到《卡里卡》聲稱它們有意地遺漏了阿卡耶迦（Akhayikas），即我們的《經文》第四卷中所含的說明故事時，這不能証明它們相較於我們現有的《經文》的後出性；但這表明在伊瓦拉‧奎什瓦（Isvara-Krishna）的時代，存在著一個數論哲學的理論體系，其中包含了我們在現代《經文》中所發現的這類故事，但既不在《卡里卡》中也不在《真諦摘要》（Tattvasamasa）中。除了這些故事，伊瓦拉‧奎什瓦還遺漏了其他事物，這些被納入帕拉瓦達（Paravada）的名稱之下，大概是指諸如伊瓦拉（Isvara）之必然性這樣的爭議。

在這些情況下，我敢說這樣一部經文形式的著作不僅曾經存在過，而且我們實際上已經掌握了它，即那部備受忽視的《諦諦婆薩摩娑》（Tattva-samasa）文本。因為它包含了許多新的技術術語，它立即被認定為現代之作，彷彿對於我們來說是新的東西在時間順序上也必然是新的。我們對於數論派（Samkhya）的歷史瞭解甚少，不足以為如此有把握的結論提供合理的依據。科爾布魯克¹很久以前曾告訴我們，如果可以相信卡皮拉²的注疏家（而我們為何不該相信呢？），《諦諦婆薩摩娑》正是數論哲學的適當教科書。只是出於偶然，他科爾布魯克找不到它的抄本。³他寫道：「《諦諦婆薩摩娑》是否現存於卡皮拉，或者《般若耶西卡經》（Sutras of Pañcasikha）是否現存，皆不確定。」又寫道：「從《卡皮拉註疏》（Kapila-bhāshya）的序言看來，一部更簡明扼要的經文或格言形式的著作，題名為《諦諦婆薩摩娑》，並歸為同一作者，即卡皮拉所作。」
我承認本論文的導言部分聽起來是現代的，很可能確實如此，但我找不到其他的標記。¹ --- ¹ 《論文集》第一卷，第244頁。

在著作的「本體」中沒有現代日期。相反地，其中有若干跡象表明它是比我們所擁有的《數論頌》（Karikas）或《經》（Sutras）更早的數論哲學形式。當它與《數論頌》一致時，有時幾乎是逐字相同，似乎是韻文本預設了散文本，而非散文本預設韻文版本。在《經》本身中，我們尚未發現任何引用後來的數論文獻所特有的無神論或非神論教義的跡象，而這些教義在《數論頌》中也仍然缺失。所謂的「神力」（Aisvaryas）或超人能力在《真理要義》（Tattva-samasa）中得到認可，雖然這極為可疑，但似乎預設了對某個伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）的認可；然而《真理要義》中普魯沙（Purusha）與梵（Brahman）的直接同一性，確實指向古代數論較早且不那麼明確的無主（Nirisvara）或無主宰特性。還應該提及的是，毗濕奴笈多（Vis/nUBhikshu）在此類事務上是一位重要的權威，甚至被某些人認為本身就是我們現代《數論經》的作者，他理所當然地認為《真理要義》肯定先於我們所擁有的《迦毗羅經》（Kapila-Sutras）。那麼為什麼他要為迦毗羅辯護，而不為《真理要義》的作者辯護，使之免於「重複累述」（Punarukti）或為我們提供無用重複的指責？為什麼他除了說《迦毗羅經》既簡潔又完整，而《真理要義》既簡潔又緊湊之外，找不到《迦毗羅經》存在的其他藉口呢？
科爾布魯克因無法找到《創世論》（Tattva-samasa）的手稿，遂決定改譯《數論頌》（Samkhya-karikas），因此大多數學者便產生了一種印象，認為在印度，這部韻文版本也被視為數論哲學中最有權威性且最通俗的教科書。這就是某些成見產生的方式。自從我們從巴蘭泰因（Ballantyne）得知——他曾居住在瓦拉納西，在那裡，除了那些看過威爾遜教授的英文版本的人外，這些頌文幾乎完全不為人知。

《數論要義》。227 儘管《數論要義》在巴蘭泰恩（Ballantyne）所聘僱的所有本地助手中廣為人知。若考慮到撰寫在其上的評註數量，以及在其他評註中出現的提及頻率，我們也無法懷疑在巴蘭泰恩最熟悉的印度地區，它確實是一部重要且流行的著作。巴蘭泰恩所出版的評註，如果我正確理解他的意思，乃是匿名的。它首先提供了所謂的《數論蘇特拉》（Samkhya-Sutra），然後是《數論蘇特拉義釋》（Samkhya-sutra-Vrtti）。霍爾（Hall）第1冊第3頁引述了一部克舍瑪難陀（Kshemananda）的評註，名為《數論綱要》（Samkhya-kramadipika），但對我而言並不完全清楚這是否與巴蘭泰恩所出版的同一部著作，且我也未曾取得任何其他的手稿。

我們必須不忘記，在近代，數論哲學（Sâmkhya）在印度的若干地區已經不再流行。即使在十六世紀，毗亞那-比丘（Vijñâna-Bhikshu）在其《數論經》（Sâmkhya-Sûtras）的註疏（第五章）中就抱怨說，該學說已被時代之日所吞沒，知識之月所餘無幾；而在《薄伽梵往世書》（Bhagavata Purâṇa）第一卷第三章第十節中，數論被稱為「時間所毀」（Kâla-vipluta）。威爾遜教授告訴我，在他與印度學者的整個交往過程中，他只遇到過一位婆羅門聲稱熟悉這一哲學學派的著作；班達卡爾教授（同上，第三頁）指出，「數論傳統」（Sâmkhya-pravacana）的名字在印度的他那一地區完全無人知曉。因此，我們完全可以理解，數論的手稿在印度極為稀少，在某些地區更是完全缺失。還有一種可能是，《真理要義》（Tattva-samâsa）本身篇幅過小，這可能降低了它在印度學者眼中的地位，久而久之，它可能被其更加龐大的註疏所掩蓋。但如果我們將其視為它自己所宣稱的，以及至少在毗亞那-比丘時代在印度被認為是的那樣，在我看來，它似乎正是填補我之前提到的那個空白所需要的書。就其本身而言，它篇幅只有區區數頁。實際上，它不過是一份主題的列舉，從這個意義上說，它將與

頗為費解的「數論」(Samkhya) 這個名稱，其實不過是「列舉」(enumeration) 之意。相較於此，這個標題的其他詮釋解說似乎都過於牽強1。根據毗亞那‧比丘 (Vi<7//ana-Bhikshu) 在其對《經文》(Sutras) 的註疏中所言 (pp. 6, no, ed. Hall)，《數論經》(Sa??ikhya-Sutras) 與《瑜伽經》(Yoga-Sutras) 實際上都是《真諦總合經》(Tattva-samasa-Sutras) 的發展。兩者因此都被稱為「數論教說」(S&mkhya-pravafcana)，即數論的闡述；而瑜伽經則更增加了論證至上主 (Isvara) 或最高主宰存在的特殊論據，因此被稱為「有主數論」(Sesvara)，與被稱為「無主數論」(An-isvara，即無主宰) 的數論相對立。

在此重要的是注意到**六十教義**（Shashtfi-tantra）這個名稱，是由伊濕瓦羅-克里希納（Ísvara-Kf'ishTza）或至少是由《七論》（the 7）及其《頌文》（K&rik&s）的作者所給予的，應當出現在《實體總論》（Tattva-samasa）中並得到說明，該論包含了第六十四和六十五條中列舉的十七項，以及第六十二和六十三條中先前展示的三十三項，連同十項慕利卡爾塔（Mlilikarthas，或稱基本事實）一起，這些加總起來將構成《六十教義》的六十個主題。在《實體總論》的二十五個重大主題的結尾，我們發現了直白的宣言：「flti tattva-samas4khya-sa77ikhya-siitr^ui/」（此為稱為《實體總論》的數論經文結束處。）乍看之下，無疑地，薩摩沙（Samasa）似乎只是指一部摘要；但薩摩沙也可用來與《廣論》（Brihat）相對，而不存在其他任何作品可被稱為其摘要，當然既不是《頌文》也不是我們所擁有的現代經文。整個編排方式與其他更新近的數論哲學著作完全不同。例如，三種苦惱（通常構成整個體系的起點）被貶至最後作為一個單獨的主題。我們遇到了技術性的主題和技術性的術語，在其他似乎更加現代的數論著作中根本找不到。《實體總論》的簡短性質幾乎無法用作反對其曾經存在的論據——它們在霍爾版本的《數論正理派註疏》（Hall's edition of the Sawkhyapravafcana-bhashya）一八五六年版的前言中被提及。其中一些僅是定義，而無任何語源學的嘗試。
